content.full_text:
A-815711

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814
Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive-Legal • • Enforcement • • Statement of Economic Interest
(916) 322 5662        322 5660           322 3901          322-6641                322 6444

February 17, 1982


William M. Wilcoxen
Attorney at Law
384 Forest Avenue, Suite 24A
Laguna Beach, CA 92651

Dear Mr. Wilcoxen:

Thank you for your letter requesting advice from the
Fair Political Practices Commission. I apologize for the
delay in my reply. In your letter, you stated that you have
been appointed to serve on the Laguna Beach City Council
from July 28, 1981, to April 1982. You are also an attorney
who, prior to the City Council appointment, did consulting
work for the owner of a 10-acre parcel of land in Laguna
Beach. In this capacity, you determined that the parcel,
currently zoned for low density housing, would be most valuable if developed as a restaurant and motel. You advised
the owner that this usage would probably gain the Council's
approval. For this consulting work, you were paid over
$250. Since being appointed to the Council, you have not
done any additional work for the owner.

As a councilmember, you have been confronted with two
specific decisions involving land in the vicinity of the 10-
acre parcel. You have asked the staff whether it would be
proper for you to participate in these decisions. Additionally,
you expressed general concern about your ability to participate
in other, similar questions which might arise.

The Political Reform Act prohibits public officials
from making or participating in the making of a governmental
decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a
financial interest. (Government Code Section 87100.)1/ An

1/Hereinafter all statutory references are to the Government
Code unless otherwise indicated.

William M. Wilcoxen
February 17, 1982
Page Two


official has a "financial interest" if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material effect on
certain financial interests. (See Section 87103.)

One of the financial interests specified in Section
87103 is any source of income aggregating $250 or more in
value received by the public official within 12 months prior
to when the decision is made. (Section 87103(c).) The
owner of the 10-acre parcel is such a source of income to
you.2/ Therefore, you may not make or participate in the
making of any decisions which will foreseeably have a material
financial effect on the owner of the 10-acre parcel. (See
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702(b)(3)(C) and (D) for guidance
in determining what constitutes a "material" effect.)

The first decision involving the 10-acre parcel con-
fronting you is whether or not to allow an increase in the
capacity of the sewer lines which extend to an area of City-
owned property located approximately one mile from the 10-
acre parcel. The increased capacity would allow a nonprofit
group to use the site for its outdoor pageants and an art
festival.

Such a decision arguably could have a financial effect
on the owner of the 10-acre parcel. For instance, an increase
in the size of the water lines might save the owner from
having to pay for an increase if he constructs the motel and
restaurant. Also, encouragement of the pageant could ultimately
generate more guests for the hotel and restaurant, with a
resultant increase in revenues for the owner.

Each of these effects, however, is subject to many
contingencies. Thus it seems doubtful that Section 87103's
requirement that there be a "reasonably foreseeable" material
financial effect on the source of income is met. Therefore,
unless you are aware of additional facts which make a material
financial effect foreseeable, disqualification is not required
under Section 87100 and you could participate in such a
decision.

_______________________________________________

2/The disqualification provisions apply regardless of
whether you pay the money back to the owner.

William M. Wilcoxen
February 17, 1982
Page Three


Another decision you are confronted with as a council-
member is one on whether to allow an extension of time to a
developer who wants to purchase a parcel of a developed property.
The parcel will be developed with residential housing and is
located approximately half a mile from the 10-acre parcel.
Again although this decision may ultimately have some financial
effect on the owner of the 10-acre parcel, with its development's
distance from the parcel and the contingencies involved it
is not "reasonably foreseeable" that it will have a material
financial effect on the 10-acre parcel.

As to other decisions which might come before the City
Council, you must weigh the facts involved in each situation
to determine whether you must abstain from voting. Decisions
which directly affect the 10-acre parcel, such as a decision
to change the zoning on the 10-acre parcel, would have a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the
owner of the parcel and would require your disqualification.
If you are confronted with a situation in which you cannot
determine whether or not it would be proper for you to partici-
pate in a vote, you may request advice from the staff, forwarding
to us a letter containing all of the relevant facts.

If you have any additional questions, please feel free
to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Very truly yours,

Janis Shank McLoan
Counsel
Legal Division

JSM:plh
Enclosures

WILLIAM M. WILCOXEN
ATTORNEY AT LAW
304 FOREST AVENUE, SUITE 24A
LAGUNA BEACH, CALIFORNIA 92651
TELEPHONE (714) 494-7563

December 21, 1981


Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Attention: Barbara Milman

Dear Ms. Milman:

     I am a member of the Laguna Beach City Council,
having been appointed on July 28, 1981, to complete a term
expiring in April, 1982.

     I have talked about the facts set forth below
with Janice McClean of your office, who suggested that I
write.

     I am an attorney and have been involved in land
use matters both publicly and privately, particularly
matters relating to coastal law compliance.

     Before being appointed to the Council, I was
consulted by the owner of ten acres of land in Laguna Beach.
The owners wanted to know what could be done with their
property. The property is zoned for low density residential
use, but is at an intersection of two arterial roads.

     I advised them that the residential use was not
consistent with anybody's serious long range plans for the
area, and that a restaurant and motel use would probably
have the best chance of gaining approval.

     They paid me a fee which was more than $250.00
and less than $1,000.00. We later talked about forming a
group to take an option on the property; but that discussion
ended when I was appointed to the City Council and I told
them that I would not be further involved with the property.

Fair Political Practices Commission
December 21, 1981
Page Two

The ten acres upon which I consulted is next to
approximately five hundred acres of land owned by the City
of Laguna Beach.

There is a seventy-acre parcel that due to
topography and general consensus will remain open space.
Then there is land that is due to be used for a right of
way for a major highway. The City has already agreed to
sell the right of way to the County.

On the other side of the throughway corridor,
there is a parcel of land which the City has agreed to sell
to a developer for residential housing. It is more than
half a mile from the ten-acre site on the other side of the
highway corridor, and the developer is now seeking an extension of time on his agreement with the City. The City
needs the funds from the sale of that property to pay for
the total acreage it purchased.

The first question I am raising, although no one
else that I know of has raised it, is whether there is any
reason I cannot vote on the issue of the extension of time?

Also on the other side of the throughway corridor, approximately a mile from the ten-acre site, is another
parcel of the City's property which is being considered for
use by a non-profit group which stages outdoor pageants and
an art festival. They presently use city land about two
miles in the other direction and want to move.

The immediate issue is that water lines are being
extended to the City property, and will need to have a
larger capacity if the non-profit group is going to move.
They have offered to pay the additional cost of about
$15,000.00, but want the City to agree that if they do not
later move to the site, they will get their money back.

It seems to me that any connection between the
vote on the utility sizing and the ten-acre parcel is extremely remote, and no one has suggested that it would not
be proper to vote on it, but I would like your opinion.

So far as I know, neither vote would substantially
effect the value of the ten-acre parcel.

As I also told Janice McClean, during the brief
term of my office I would like to vote on matters coming
before the Council, and I mentioned to her that if dollars

Fair Political Practices Commission
December 21, 1981
Page Three

are the big thing, I would be willing to refund my con-
sulting fees. I had previously understood from the City
Attorney that I could vote on such matters unless the con-
sulting fees constituted a significant portion of my income.
Of course they don't, and the idea that with their consulting
fees they purchased my independent judgment on issues such
as I have presented to you is difficult for me to follow
for I know that it is not true.

I do not want my nine-month excursion into small-
town politics to diminish what I believe is a twenty-year
record of ethical law practice, and therefore seek your
opinion.

If you desire any further statement of facts,
please don't hesitate to call. I would appreciate receiving
your opinion by January 5, 1982, if at all possible, since
the Council meets on that date.

Yours truly,

[signature]
WILLIAM M. WILCOXEN

sections.question: Whether William M. Wilcoxen, as a Laguna Beach City Council member, has a financial interest that would disqualify him from voting on: (1) a decision to allow an increase in sewer line capacity to serve a non-profit group's pageants and art festival on city-owned property approximately one mile from a ten-acre parcel for which he previously consulted; and (2) a decision on an extension of time for a developer seeking to purchase city property for residential housing more than half a mile away on the other side of a highway corridor from the ten-acre parcel. He had previously consulted for the owner of the ten-acre parcel for a fee of more than $250 and advised that restaurant and motel use would gain Council approval.
sections.conclusion: The disqualification provision under Government Code Section 87100 does not apply to the first decision regarding sewer line capacity because, despite potential contingent effects, it is not 'reasonably foreseeable' that the decision will have a material financial effect on the ten-acre parcel owner. Similarly, the second decision regarding the extension of time for residential development is too remote and contingent to meet the 'reasonably foreseeable' requirement. However, decisions that directly affect the ten-acre parcel itself, such as changing its zoning, would require disqualification. Wilcoxen should analyze each future decision individually based on whether it reasonably foreseeably has a material financial effect on the property owner.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether a city council member must disqualify himself from voting on decisions that may have indirect or remote financial effects on a property owner for whom he previously provided consulting work.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC advises that disqualification is not required for the two specific decisions presented because their effects on the ten-acre parcel are too remote and contingent to be 'reasonably foreseeable' as materially financial. However, any decision directly affecting the ten-acre parcel would require disqualification, and Wilcoxen must evaluate each future matter individually to determine whether abstention is necessary.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is incoming request letter dated December 21, 1981, followed by FPPC response letter dated February 17, 1982. Text quality is degraded with OCR errors but main content is discernible. The verbatim question and conclusion sections are extremely long and complex, encompassing multiple issues. Extraction confidence is high for the overall advisory structure and conclusions despite OCR quality issues.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether William M. Wilcoxen, as a Laguna Beach City Council member, has a financial interest that would disqualify him from voting on: (1) a decision to allow an increase in sewer line capacity to serve a non-profit group's pageants and art festival on city-owned property approximately one mile from a ten-acre parcel for which he previously consulted; and (2) a decision on an extension of time for a developer seeking to purchase city property for residential housing more than half a mile away on the other side of a highway corridor from the ten-acre parcel. He had previously consulted for the owner of the ten-acre parcel for a fee of more than $250 and advised that restaurant and motel use would gain Council approval.

CONCLUSION: The disqualification provision under Government Code Section 87100 does not apply to the first decision regarding sewer line capacity because, despite potential contingent effects, it is not 'reasonably foreseeable' that the decision will have a material financial effect on the ten-acre parcel owner. Similarly, the second decision regarding the extension of time for residential development is too remote and contingent to meet the 'reasonably foreseeable' requirement. However, decisions that directly affect the ten-acre parcel itself, such as changing its zoning, would require disqualification. Wilcoxen should analyze each future decision individually based on whether it reasonably foreseeably has a material financial effect on the property owner.

embedding.summary: This is an FPPC advice letter responding to William M. Wilcoxen's inquiry regarding potential conflicts of interest arising from his consulting relationship with a ten-acre property owner while serving on the Laguna Beach City Council. The FPPC concludes that two specific decisions involving nearby city property do not require his disqualification because the financial effects are not reasonably foreseeable as material, but directs him to evaluate future decisions on a case-by-case basis.
